sections.title: Fats
sections.narration: 0.3g of fat. omega 3 is doing a lot of the work here. for a vegetable, nutrient payoff is the whole point.
sections.subtitleText: 0.3g of fat. omega 3 is doing a lot of the work here. for a vegetable, nutrient payoff is the whole point.

sections.title: Carbs
sections.narration: 9g of carbs. fibre is doing a lot of the work here. for vegetables, low downside is useful, but there still needs to be real payoff.
sections.subtitleText: 9g of carbs. fibre is doing a lot of the work here. for vegetables, low downside is useful, but there still needs to be real payoff.

sections.title: Proteins
sections.narration: 3.4g of protein. bioavailability is one of the best parts of the protein story. protein is not the main pitch here, but extra support still matters.
sections.subtitleText: 3.4g of protein. bioavailability is one of the best parts of the protein story. protein is not the main pitch here, but extra support still matters.

sections.title: Vitamins
sections.narration: vitamin k at 148% daily value, vitamin c at 94% daily value, vitamin a at 15% daily value stand out most. that is major vitamin support.
sections.subtitleText: vitamin k at 148% daily value, vitamin c at 94% daily value, vitamin a at 15% daily value stand out most. that is major vitamin support.

sections.title: Minerals
sections.narration: potassium at 11% daily value stands out most. useful, but still pretty limited.
sections.subtitleText: potassium at 11% daily value stands out most. useful, but still pretty limited.

sections.title: Pros
sections.narration: pros first: makes meals feel more balanced. the category has real value beyond single-nutrient stats. good volume for the calories. that matters for satiety and general usefulness. very low downside whole food. vegetables win a lot simply by offering clean nutritional payoff.

sections.title: Cons
sections.narration: the drawbacks next: taste and texture can limit compliance. a strong paper profile still needs real-world repeatability. energy density is very low. good for some use cases, but not enough on its own. usually needs pairing to make a full meal. most vegetables are not complete by themselves.

closing.summary: Brussels Sprouts has a genuinely strong overall case. the practical downsides are real, but they do not outweigh the upside. best when you want low downside and easy micronutrient support.
closing.useCaseNote: Best when you want low downside and easy micronutrient support.
closing.cta: Would you rank it the same, or nah?

explanation.summary: Carbs are carrying this food most, while minerals are the biggest drag. It lands in S tier.
